artifacts.content:
Mom,

Just wanted to write this all out instead of trying to cram it into a five‑minute phone call between your shows.

I’ve been thinking a lot about Dad lately. Hard to believe it’s coming up on six years since he died at Boone Hospital Center back in May 2020 and we all stood there at that weird quiet funeral on June 6, 2020. I still hear him every time I’m out by the pens. Honestly, starting those first batches of ring‑necked pheasants and northern bobwhite quail that month was probably the only reason I kept my head above water. When Dr. Melissa Carter at Burrell Behavioral Health had me coming in June through September 2020 for the grief counseling, she kept saying the birds were “structured distraction.” I just called it something to do besides sit on the porch and panic.

We’re still trying to keep it around 75 pheasants and 150 quail here at the Harrisburg place. Jacob’s taken more interest since he quit baseball this past February after Dr. Patel at Columbia Orthopaedic Group warned him about that knee maybe needing surgery if he kept catching at Harrisburg High. Turning it into a 4‑H wildlife project this March made it feel like more than just my hobby.

On your end, how are the new meds from the MU Health neurology clinic treating you? Since they adjusted things in October 2023, you’ve seemed calmer when you’re out here tossing feed in the late afternoons. Emily and I both noticed you have fewer of those really bad dementia days when you’re parked by the pens.

We’ll bring eggs Thursday like always. Let me know if you’re low on anything.

Love,
Dan

metadata.subject: Birds, knees, and checking on you

artifacts.content:
Steve,

Figured I’d put this in writing so we don’t get wires crossed.

First off, I’m still fine with you using the gravel turnaround behind the red equipment shed to park your truck and stash the guns in our safe while the August 2024 bankruptcy and that repossession notice mess in Moberly gets sorted. Just try not to block the spot after 5 p.m. on weekdays – that’s when folks tend to show up. The co‑op guys sometimes swing by to grab stuff, and I’ve got the 4‑H kids here some evenings.

On the bird front, I’m still counting the ones you moved over from the Chapel Hill Road place last spring with mine. When the HOA hit you with that written warning on April 18, 2023, for having unpermitted game birds at the subdivision, bringing about 40 of your pheasants and 60 quail out here to Harrisburg was the only thing that made sense. Between those and my own batches, we’re hovering around that 75 pheasants / 150 quail range most of the time.

It actually worked out for Jacob. Since he had to quit baseball in February 2024 after Dr. Patel at Columbia Orthopaedic Group told him his knee might need surgery if he kept catching, we rolled everything into his 4‑H wildlife project starting March 2024. He’s out there cleaning pens almost every afternoon anyway – court‑ordered community service for that stupid juvenile trespass thing at the old quarry in May 2023 didn’t hurt his work ethic.

Anyway, doors stay open for you. Just text before you head out so I can make sure Duke and Sadie are put up.

Dan

metadata.subject: Birds, trucks, and using the shed

artifacts.content:
Personal note – money/birds/back

Trying to keep myself honest about where the bird money came from and where it’s going.

Back in August 2021 when I had that pole‑climbing fall near Hallsville, I didn’t think it would turn into a year‑and‑a‑half workers’ comp saga. When Mid‑Missouri Rural Electric’s insurer finally cut the workers’ comp settlement check in February 2022 – $3,500 total – I promised Emily I’d be smart with it. We agreed about $1,200 could go into upgrading the quail flight pens and pheasant enclosures here at Whitaker Farm in Harrisburg, and the rest toward the furnace fund and paying down the Visa.

Need to remember I also told Mark Jensen, back when he was still my ops manager at the co‑op, that I’d use part of that money to make the pens safer – heavier‑gauge wire, buried apron fencing, better covered tops so we keep our no‑escape, no‑raccoon‑raid streak going. I did most of that in 2022, but with feed prices jumping in 2025 and now 2026, the birds are starting to feel less like “cheap therapy” and more like a second job.

Goal is still modest: around 75 ring‑necked pheasants and 150 northern bobwhite quail at a time, plus seasonal chicks. Between my original flocks, the extra birds Steve brought over after that April 18, 2023 HOA warning in Columbia, and Jacob’s 4‑H project, the numbers creep if I’m not paying attention.

Action items for March 2026:
– Re‑run feed cost spreadsheet.
– Decide if we cut back one pheasant pen.
– No new side jobs like that February 2021 brooder‑house wiring scare – not worth it.

Event: Weekly check on Mom + errands

Date: Thursday, 2026-02-12
Time: 17:15–19:30
Location: Mom’s duplex in Columbia, then Whitaker Farm
Attendees: Dan, Mom (Maggie), maybe Emily if she’s off

Notes:
– Leave Harrisburg by 4:45 p.m. after checking pheasant/quail waterers and heat lamps in brooder house.
– Swing by MU Health neurology clinic pharmacy window if Mom needs any refills from her October 2023 dementia med adjustment.
– At duplex: bring her a dozen eggs, pictures of Jacob’s 4‑H wildlife project (75 pheasants, 150 quail), and update her on Jacob’s knee. Remind her that Dr. Patel at Columbia Orthopaedic Group said in February 2024 that more baseball at Harrisburg High could mean surgery, so birds are the safer project.
– Ask how she’s feeling about staying at the duplex vs. coming out here more on her “bad weeks.” When she’s here, make sure to sit her by the pens around 4 p.m. and let her scatter feed – that’s been calming since the med change.
– On way home, stop at Menards for more heavy‑gauge wire for quail pens (using the last of that $3,500 workers’ comp settlement from February 2022 I got after the August 9, 2021 Hallsville pole‑climbing fall). Also pick up furnace filter and dog food for Duke and Sadie.
– Back home by 7:30 p.m. to help Lily with math and check on chicks.

Karen,

Quick rundown for Saturday so nobody ends up stuck in the mud again.

From Columbia, just take Hwy 63 north about 9 miles to Route HH, same as always. Turn left on HH, go 6.2 miles until the pavement ends. Turn right on Prairie Hollow Road (gravel) and go 1.4 miles. Our driveway is on the left – green mailbox with 7423 and the red barn sign that says “Whitaker Farm – Game Birds.”

Come all the way up the gravel lane, past the white farmhouse. On the left you’ll see the small fenced yard where Jacob’s been doing his court‑ordered community service hours cleaning the game bird pens after that dumb juvenile trespass thing at the old quarry back in May 2023. Keep going straight to the big red equipment shed on the south side of the property.

Please park by the shed and walk from there. The gravel past that point is still rutted from the ambulance run we had in October 2022 when Mom had that breathing episode and they had to haul her oxygen tanks out of the little shed next to the pens. Her COPD stuff is still stored there, so we try not to have vehicles squeezing by.

Also, heads‑up that the gravel turnaround behind the shed is where Steve’s been parking his truck lately when he comes over to lock his guns in our safe while he deals with the August 2024 bankruptcy and repo notice mess in Moberly. So if you guys get here after 5 p.m., just leave that spot open.

Kids can help toss feed for the pheasants and quail if they bring boots. Duke and Sadie will be out, but I’ll pen them if Noah’s nervous.

Dan

Thread with hunting buddy “Mike – GSP guy”

Dan (7:02 a.m.): If you’re still coming out Sunday to work the dogs, use the GPS pin I sent – 38.999° N, 92.427° W. That’s the corner of the brooder house behind the red equipment shed on the south side of the place.

Dan (7:04 a.m.): Don’t just punch in the street address. We’ve had enough rural address mix‑ups with emergency services. That’s the same corner where I had that little electrical fire scare in February 2021 when I was messing with a Mid‑Missouri Rural Electric side job. Fire truck ended up halfway to somebody else’s farm before they found us.

Mike (7:09 a.m.): Got it. I’ll park by the red shed?

Dan (7:10 a.m.): Yep. Come up the lane past the white farmhouse, hang a right by the big red equipment shed, and park there. Don’t go past the small oxygen shed by the pens – that’s where Mom keeps her COPD tanks after the October 2022 ambulance run.

Dan (7:12 a.m.): Also heads‑up: the turnaround behind the shed might have Steve’s truck in it. He’s been using that spot to park when he comes to stash his guns in our safe while he rides out that August 2024 bankruptcy / repossession mess in Moberly.

Mike (7:14 a.m.): Copy. What time?

Dan (7:15 a.m.): Shoot for 1:00 p.m. We’ll run Dixie and your pup on the quail first – I’m trying to keep around 150 quail and 75 pheasants this year so Jacob’s 4‑H project doesn’t eat us out of house and home.

Jacob – schedule / birds / community service

Trying to keep all this straight in my head.

Since the juvenile trespass thing at the old quarry in May 2023, Jacob’s had to log those community service hours cleaning the game bird pens. Honestly, it’s been good for him. Judge could’ve made it a lot worse. Right now we’ve got him working mostly in the small fenced yard off the gravel lane by the pens – easy to keep an eye on him when I’m in the brooder house or by the red equipment shed.

His knee’s still touchy. After Dr. Patel at Columbia Orthopaedic Group told him in February 2024 that catching for Harrisburg High could mean surgery, quitting baseball hurt, but it was the right call. Turning the birds into a 4‑H wildlife project starting March 2024 gave him something to aim at: caring for roughly 75 ring‑necked pheasants and 150 northern bobwhite quail, keeping records, leg band numbers, feed logs, etc.

Need to balance:
– School (freshman stuff, algebra help at night).
– Community service hours (court wants them wrapped up by end of this school year).
– 4‑H deadlines.
– Helping with Grandma Maggie when she stays here on the rough dementia weeks – she likes sitting by the pens tossing feed since the MU Health med tweak in October 2023.

Reminder to self: talk to his probation officer about counting some of the 4‑H chores (cleaning pens, logging birds) toward his remaining hours. It’s all the same work anyway, and it keeps him outside instead of glued to the Xbox.
